title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Philosophy of Sportsmanship: Beyond the Scoreboard
content.subtitle: Why the ethical conduct of athletes is a vital component of the human experience.
content.text: In the high-stakes world of professional sports, where millions of dollars and national pride are often on the line, the concept of 'sportsmanship' is frequently viewed as a quaint, secondary concern. We are taught that 'winning is everything' and that any advantage, no matter how ethically dubious, is justified by the final score. However, a deeper philosophical analysis suggests that sportsmanship is not just about 'playing fair'; it is a fundamental expression of human virtue. [31] The writer suggests that sports serve as a microcosm of society, where the rules of the game act as a voluntary social contract that allows for the pursuit of excellence within a framework of mutual respect. [/31] When an athlete breaks that contract, they are not just cheating an opponent; they are undermining the very activity they claim to love. || Central to this idea is the concept of 'The Spirit of the Game.' While the rules provide a literal boundary for behavior, the spirit involves an unwritten commitment to the integrity of the contest. [32] For example, an athlete who admits to a foul that the referee missed is prioritizing the truth of the competition over the benefit of the outcome. [/32] This level of integrity is increasingly rare in an era of 'VAR' and constant surveillance, where athletes often feel that if they aren't caught, they haven't done anything wrong. This shift from internal conscience to external policing has profound implications for the moral development of young fans who look to these athletes as role models. || [33] The author's tone when discussing the 'win-at-all-costs' culture is one of critical concern. [/33] When we prioritize results over process, we transform sport from a meaningful human endeavor into a transactional performance. This can lead to 'moral disengagement,' where athletes justify cheating, doping, or unsportsmanlike conduct as 'part of the game.' The danger is that this mindset does not stay on the field; it bleeds into other areas of life, from business to politics, fostering a culture where the ends always justify the means. || [34] Interestingly, some philosophers argue that sportsmanship is actually a form of 'aesthetic appreciation.' [/34] They suggest that we value sports because they represent the highest form of human physical and mental capability. When an athlete cheats, they disrupt the 'beauty' of that performance, turning a display of skill into a display of deception. True sportsmanship, therefore, is an act of preserving the aesthetic value of the sport itself. It is the realization that a victory achieved through dishonorable means is not a victory at all, but a hollow imitation of one. || [35] However, critics of traditional sportsmanship argue that the concept is often used to maintain the status quo and silence athletes from marginalized groups. [/35] They suggest that 'etiquette' and 'professionalism' are sometimes used as tools for control, discouraging athletes from speaking out about social injustices. From this perspective, a 'true sportsman' might be someone who has the courage to disrupt the game for the sake of a higher moral principle. This challenge forces us to reconsider the definition of sportsmanship in a modern, politically charged context. || Ultimately, the value of sport lies in its ability to test the human character. [36] The writer concludes that the ultimate purpose of competition is not to defeat an opponent, but to find a better version of oneself through the challenge. [/36] By adhering to the principles of sportsmanship, athletes transform a mere physical contest into a journey of self-discovery. Whether on the track, the court, or the pitch, the way we play the game is a clearer indication of our character than the trophies we collect. The scoreboard may tell us who won, but the conduct tells us who we are.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the writer suggests that the rules of a sport...
options: A) are an unnecessary restriction on an athlete's pursuit of excellence.
B) function as a social contract based on mutual respect.
C) are only relevant when large amounts of money are involved.
D) are designed to help athletes win at all costs.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31], which mentions the rules acting as a 'voluntary social contract.' A is the opposite of the author's point. C and D are common views the author argues against.

questions.question: The example of an athlete admitting to a missed foul illustrates...
options: A) the need for more advanced surveillance technology in sports.
B) a lack of professional ambition in modern competition.
C) the prioritization of the competition's integrity over winning.
D) a misunderstanding of the role of the referee.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32] and the mention of prioritizing 'the truth of the competition over the benefit of the outcome.' A, B, and D are not the author's point.

questions.question: What is the author's main concern regarding 'moral disengagement'?
options: A) That it will make athletes too tired to perform at their best.
B) That it will lead to a decrease in the number of fans watching sports.
C) That it will encourage a culture where outcomes are more important than ethics.
D) That it is only possible in professional sports, not in amateur ones.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and the concern that 'ends always justify the means' in all areas of life. A and B are not mentioned. D is not a distinction made in the text.

questions.question: The fourth paragraph suggests that cheating is problematic because it...
options: A) makes the sport too difficult for spectators to follow.
B) reduces the physical fitness of the athletes involved.
C) ruins the aesthetic beauty of a high-level performance.
D) is the only way for untalented athletes to win.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [34] and the mention of disrupting the 'beauty' and turning skill into 'deception.' A, B, and D are not the focus of this philosophical argument.

questions.question: According to the fifth paragraph, what is a criticism of 'traditional sportsmanship'?
options: A) It is too easy to follow and does not provide enough of a challenge.
B) it can be used to prevent athletes from addressing social issues.
C) It is no longer relevant in a world dominated by social media.
D) It favors athletes who are from wealthy backgrounds.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [35] and the idea that 'etiquette' can be used to 'silence athletes from marginalized groups.' A, C, and D are not mentioned in this context.

questions.question: Which of the following best summarizes the writer's conclusion?
options: A) The scoreboard is the only true measure of an athlete's success.
B) Athletes should focus on collecting as many trophies as possible.
C) Competition is a tool for personal growth and self-discovery.
D) Sportsmanship is only important when an opponent is watching.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36] and the idea of finding a 'better version of oneself through the challenge.' A and B are explicitly rejected. D is contradicted by the author's call for an 'internal conscience'.
